topic.what: Landmark Lancet meta-analysis comparing 110 placebo-controlled homeopathy trials matched to 110 conventional medicine trials, finding that homeopathy effects are consistent with placebo and that apparent efficacy correlates with methodological quality deficits.
topic.how: Systematic identification and matching of homeopathy and conventional medicine RCTs; regression analysis of effect sizes against trial quality indicators; examination of the quality gradient to assess publication bias and methodological confounding.
topic.why_matters: Provides the highest-quality evidence that homeopathy lacks specific clinical effects beyond placebo — directly relevant to gastritis care where homeopathic remedies are marketed as treatments, and foundational to distinguishing evidence-based from non-evidence-based complementary medicine for GI conditions.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Overall treatment effect of homeopathy relative to placebo across 110 matched trial pairs
characteristics.value: No clinically or statistically significant effect beyond placebo in high-quality trials
characteristics.evidence: Meta-analytic pooling of 110 homeopathy trials; effect disappears when restricted to larger, higher-quality trials
characteristics.context: Landmark finding published in The Lancet; widely cited as definitive evidence against homeopathy specificity

characteristics.what_it_measures: Correlation between trial methodological quality and reported homeopathy effect size, used to assess bias
characteristics.value: Lower-quality trials show larger apparent homeopathy effects; high-quality trials show effects indistinguishable from placebo
characteristics.vs_baseline: High-quality conventional medicine trials (which retain effects under quality stratification)
characteristics.evidence: Regression analysis of effect size vs trial quality score across 110 homeopathy trial pairs
characteristics.context: Quality gradient pattern is the opposite for conventional medicine trials, strengthening the contrast

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which selective publication of positive homeopathy trials inflates apparent efficacy
characteristics.value: Publication bias identified as a significant contributor to apparent homeopathy efficacy in the full dataset
characteristics.vs_baseline: Expected funnel plot symmetry under no bias
characteristics.evidence: Funnel plot asymmetry and regression analyses for small-study effects
characteristics.context: Consistent with broader literature on publication bias in complementary medicine trials

characteristics.what_it_measures: Implication of findings for gastritis patients considering homeopathic treatment
characteristics.value: No evidence basis for homeopathy in gastritis; risk of delayed effective treatment if used as substitute
characteristics.vs_baseline: Evidence-based gastritis management (H. pylori eradication, PPIs)
characteristics.evidence: Generalization from overall homeopathy evidence base; no gastritis-specific trials in primary analysis
characteristics.context: Gastroenterology tagged for relevance to KB domain; not explicitly studied in this paper

good_for: Evidence-based critiques of homeopathy in gastritis and GI medicine
Educating patients on distinction between placebo effects and specific treatment effects
Establishing a methodological benchmark for evaluating complementary medicine RCT quality
Policy decisions on inclusion of homeopathy in GI treatment guidelines
not_for: Evaluating specific herbal or nutraceutical interventions with pharmacological mechanisms (ginger, Aloe vera, ashwagandha)
Generalizing to all complementary medicine — findings specific to homeopathy
Dismissing placebo effects, which have genuine clinical utility in symptom management
requires: Distinguishing homeopathy (ultra-dilution, no active molecules) from herbal/botanical medicine with measurable phytochemical activity
Critical appraisal of trial quality when evaluating any complementary therapy
Patient communication that addresses both evidence standards and symptom relief needs

novelty: First large-scale matched meta-analysis directly comparing homeopathy and conventional medicine trial evidence bases simultaneously
Demonstrated the quality gradient as the mechanism explaining apparent homeopathy efficacy in low-quality trials
Provided a replicable methodological framework for comparative evidence assessment across alternative medicine modalities
open_problems: Mechanism of placebo response in GI conditions like gastritis remains an area of active research with clinical utility implications
Distinguishing homeopathy from other CAM modalities with genuine pharmacological activity (e.g., herbal medicine) is poorly understood by patients and some clinicians
Ethical frameworks for offering placebo-only treatments in patient-centered care are still debated
Whether high-quality homeopathy-specific trials (particularly for GI indications) exist that were not captured remains a gap
